title: Should You Start Your Indian Startup Outside Bangalore?
summary: Nearly half of India's new startups launch outside Bangalore, cutting costs 40-70%. But VC access still favors metros. Here's who should actually move.
content:
Nearly half of India's new startups now come from cities that aren't Bangalore, Mumbai, or Delhi. That shift didn't happen by accident. For most early-stage founders, starting outside Bangalore is worth it: you'll run leaner, access underserved markets, and stretch your runway significantly further. The exception is founders who need institutional VC in their first year. That's still harder from a Tier 2 city, and it's worth being honest about that upfront.

Key Takeaways

  • 48% of India's DPIIT-recognized startups now launch from Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities, up from roughly 35% in 2020.
  • Operating costs in cities like Jaipur run 60 to 70% lower than Bangalore for equivalent office space and technical roles.
  • Around 75 to 80% of institutional venture capital still flows to Bangalore, Mumbai, and Delhi-NCR.
  • B2B SaaS, agritech, and fintech for non-metro users fit Tier 2 well; metro-focused consumer apps usually don't.
  • Pune is the most practical middle ground: lower costs than Bangalore with meaningfully stronger investor access than most non-metro cities.

Should you actually start outside Bangalore?

For most bootstrapped or pre-seed founders in India, yes. You'll run 40 to 70 percent leaner, find motivated talent at lower salary bands, and stretch your runway on the same seed check. The one solid reason to stay: if your plan requires institutional VC within 12 months, that network is still hard to replicate remotely.

That said, the 2026 picture is meaningfully different from three years ago. Funding for Tier 2 and Tier 3 startups hit $1.2 billion in H1 2025, a 55 percent jump year on year. Investors are moving toward these ecosystems. Slowly, but they're moving.

High-burn startups also faced valuation discounts of 40 to 60 percent in 2024 to 2026 compared to startups with healthier runways. That's one more reason the leaner Tier 2 operating model fits the current investor mood better than it did a few years ago.

What do the costs actually look like?

Operating costs in Jaipur or Indore run 60 to 70 percent lower than Bangalore for similar office space and a comparable team. Pune is 35 to 45 percent cheaper. On a seed check of ₹50 lakh, that difference can mean 8 months of runway or 18. That's not a small thing.

The gap compounds once you factor in salaries. Junior engineers in Jaipur typically earn 30 to 40 percent less than Bangalore equivalents for the same role. A 10-person team burning ₹15 lakh a month in Bangalore might run the same setup for ₹5 to 7 lakh in Jaipur or Indore.

FactorBangalorePuneJaipurIndore
Cost vs BangaloreBaseline~35-45% lower~60-70% lower~60-70% lower
Investor accessHighMediumLow-mediumLow
Govt startup supportModerateStrongStrong (iStart)Moderate
Senior tech talentDeepMediumLimitedLimited
Recent funding momentumDominant$395M+ (2024)Growing steadilyEarly-stage focus

What do you actually give up by leaving Bangalore?

Mainly two things: investor proximity and access to senior specialized talent. Around 75 to 80 percent of India's institutional venture capital still flows through the top three metros. Senior engineers with 8 or more years of specialized experience are thinner outside those cities. Both gaps are manageable, but they require deliberate planning to work around.

Investor proximity is the harder problem. Most VC partners still prefer to meet founders in person before writing a check. This pattern has occured consistently enough across Tier 2 founder stories that it's worth accounting for in your timeline: expect more travel and calendar friction, plus a fundraising process that runs longer than you'd budget for.

Senior specialized talent is the second real constraint. At the begining, when your team is small and the work is core product development, you won't feel it much. But if you eventually need a senior distributed-systems engineer or a product leader with a decade of experience, that hire is coming from a metro, with a relocation package.

Which startup types fit Tier 2 best?

B2B SaaS for small and medium businesses, agritech, edtech built for non-metro learners, and fintech targeting daily-wage earners all fit naturally. Your customers are nearby and your team understands them from the inside. A consumer social app competing for urban attention is a harder sell from Jaipur, and forcing the fit doesn't work.

The structural advantage of Tier 2 is proximity to India's next billion users. Most of them don't live in Koramangala. Founders embedded in these ecosystems often catch real-world problems earlier than metro founders researching from a distance, and that gap shows up in the product.

High-burn startups planning for multiple funding rounds are a worse fit unless they're willing to establish metro presence by Series A. Investors who back Tier 2 founders still expect a path toward scale, which usually means a metro-facing operation at some stage.

Which cities are worth looking at in 2026?

Pune first. It had $395 million in startup funding in 2024, including a $15 million Series B for SuperGaming. Strong engineering colleges, a short flight from Mumbai's VC community, and meaningfully lower costs than Bangalore make it the lowest-risk alternative for most founders.

Jaipur second, for maximum cost efficiency and state support. The iStart Rajasthan program has registered more than 7,100 startups across the state. Celebal Technologies, an enterprise AI firm, raised a $15 million Series B from Jaipur. State subsidies on office space and seed grants are real and accessible, not just on paper.

Indore for patient early-stage founders. The fintech and enterprise software clusters are nascent but active. Costs are low, student talent is motivated, and competition for that talent is still thin. It's the kind of market some founders position themselves in a full cycle early.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can you raise VC funding from a Tier 2 city in India?

Yes, but expect more friction. Around 75 to 80 percent of institutional venture capital flows through Bangalore, Mumbai, and Delhi-NCR. Tier 2 city startup funding hit $1.2 billion in H1 2025, mostly angel and seed rounds. Series A and later rounds usually require regular metro presence from the founding team.

Is Pune actually cheaper than Bangalore for startups?

Yes, meaningfully. Operating costs in Pune run 35 to 45 percent lower than comparable setups in Bangalore, covering office space, engineering salaries, and general overhead. You trade some investor proximity for those savings. For most bootstrapped or angel-funded founders, that trade is worth making.

What is iStart Rajasthan and is it worth registering?

iStart is Rajasthan's state-backed startup platform. It gives registered startups access to subsidized co-working space, seed grants, mentorship, and procurement preferences with state agencies. Over 7,100 startups have registered across the state. If you're building in Rajasthan, registration is free and the practical benefits are real.

Which startup types should not move to a Tier 2 city?

Metro-focused consumer apps, deep tech startups needing senior specialized engineers, fintech requiring deep regulatory relationships in Mumbai, and any startup planning to raise a Series A within 12 months without existing investor relationships. These depend on metro proximity too heavily for the cost savings to offset the added friction.

How much can a startup realistically save by moving from Bangalore to Jaipur?

A 10-person team burning roughly ₹15 lakh a month in Bangalore could run a similar setup for ₹5 to 7 lakh in Jaipur. That extends an 8-month runway to 17 to 22 months on the same seed capital. The actual number depends on roles and seniority, but the directional savings are consistently large.

Does starting outside Bangalore hurt credibility with investors?

Less than it used to. Enterprise and B2B startups from Jaipur and Pune now raise institutional rounds without city-related discounts. Consumer startups targeting metro users still face some bias from certain investors. The credibility gap has narrowed since 2022, but it hasn't fully gone away.

The Short Version

If you're bootstrapped or pre-seed, building for non-metro Indian users, and have at least 18 months before needing a serious fundraising round, starting outside Bangalore is almost certainly the smarter move in 2026. You'll last longer, build leaner habits, and understand the market you're actually serving. Start in Bangalore if you genuinely need that VC network immediately, or if your product requires that specific talent density. Both are valid reasons. Just be honest about which one actually applies to you.
